title: The Fourth Sunday in Lent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## March 14, 2021 | Preacher: Richard Lawson

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

March 14, 2021 was the Fourth Sunday in Lent, also known as Laetare Sunday (from the Latin "rejoice"). Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would have included John 3:14-21 (the Nicodemus passage and John 3:16) as the Gospel, with Old Testament reading from Numbers 21:4-9 (the bronze serpent) and Psalm 107:1-3, 17-22. This marked the midpoint of the Lenten season, traditionally a Sunday of some relief and hope before the final push toward Holy Week.

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the early stages of pandemic recovery, with many dioceses still operating under COVID-19 restrictions on in-person worship. Bishop Robert J. O'Neill was serving the Diocese of Colorado at this time. The broader church was grappling with how to safely resume full congregational singing and in-person communion, issues that would dominate Episcopal Church discussions through spring 2021. No major General Convention actions were scheduled for this date, as the 80th General Convention had been postponed from summer 2021 to July 2022 due to the pandemic.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Colorado was experiencing early spring weather patterns with moderate temperatures in the 50s-60s Fahrenheit. The state was in the latter stages of vaccine rollout to vulnerable populations, with Colorado having administered over 1 million vaccine doses by mid-March. Locally, Denver's Capitol Hill neighborhood remained quieter than usual due to ongoing pandemic restrictions on restaurants and bars. The Colorado legislature was in session (it runs January-early June), and debates over pandemic recovery and budget issues were active.

NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The United States was at a critical juncture in the pandemic response: the vaccination campaign was accelerating with the Pfizer, Moderna, and newly authorized Johnson & Johnson vaccines rolling out to broader populations, though supply remained inconsistent. President Biden had been in office for nearly two months and had signed major legislation including the $1.9 trillion American Rescue Plan stimulus bill (passed by Congress March 10, 2021, signed March 11). The country was experiencing a sense of cautious optimism about pandemic recovery, with cases declining from the winter surge. The Derek Chauvin trial for the death of George Floyd was ongoing in Minneapolis, having begun jury selection in late February.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The COVID-19 pandemic remained the dominant international story, with Europe experiencing a third wave of infections and vaccine rollouts progressing unevenly across nations. India was beginning to experience its devastating second wave of infections, though the full scale would not become apparent until later in March. The Myanmar military coup (February 1, 2021) was still a major story, with Aung San Suu Kyi's trial underway and ongoing protests and civil disobedience.

CULTURAL MOMENT

American society was experiencing a transitional moment between grief and hope: vaccine availability was creating genuine optimism about pandemic recovery, yet the trauma of the previous year remained fresh. There was growing cultural reckoning around racial justice following Floyd's death, reflected in media, education, and institutional conversations nationwide. Sports were returning to near-normalcy with March Madness beginning on March 15-16 (immediately after this sermon), and the Denver Nuggets were in active NBA play. A sense of cautious reopening dominated: schools were beginning hybrid or full in-person instruction, restaurants were beginning to open dining rooms at limited capacity, and Americans were planning vacations and family gatherings for spring and summer for the first time in over a year.
